transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hello, Alex. I'm glad you're here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Hi. Thanks for having me. I guess... I’ve been thinking a lot about how things seem to spiral out of control sometimes. Like, if I mess up one little thing, it feels like the whole day is ruined. I don’t know, maybe it sounds silly, but it’s frustrating. I just want to get things right, you know?

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you're feeling a lot of pressure when things don’t go as planned, and that can be really frustrating. You mentioned that if you mess up one little thing, it feels like the whole day is ruined. Can you tell me more about what that experience is like for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, it’s just... when I wake up late or something like that, my mind goes straight to this place where I think, “Why can’t I even do this right?” It’s like I can’t shake that feeling, and then I start to feel anxious and worried. I guess I just feel like I should have it all together, but then I don’t. It makes me want to just give up on the day entirely. It’s hard to explain, but it’s like a weight that just keeps building.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds really heavy to carry that weight of expectation and self-criticism, especially when things don’t go as planned. You mentioned feeling anxious and worried when you wake up late, and that it leads you to think you should have everything together. I’m curious, where do you think that expectation comes from for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I’m not really sure. I guess it’s just something I’ve always felt, like there’s this standard I’m supposed to meet. Maybe it’s from... I don’t know, just wanting to be successful or not let people down? It feels like everyone else has it figured out, and I’m just... falling behind. It’s hard to shake that feeling, and I think it makes me more critical of myself when things go wrong.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like there's a strong sense of wanting to meet certain standards, perhaps tied to your desire for success and not wanting to disappoint others. That can create a lot of pressure, especially when you feel like everyone else has it figured out. It’s understandable that this would lead to being more critical of yourself when things don’t go as planned. How do you usually cope with those feelings when they arise?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Well, I guess my coping isn’t the healthiest. When I feel that pressure, I sometimes just... avoid everything. Like, if I wake up late, I might call in sick and just try to escape it all. I’ve also turned to drinking, which probably isn’t great either. It’s like I’m trying to numb the feelings, but then it just leads to more problems, like fights with my girlfriend. I know it’s not the best way to handle things, but in the moment, it feels like the only option.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re aware that your coping strategies, like avoiding responsibilities and turning to drinking, aren’t the healthiest choices, but they feel like the only options in those moments of pressure. That must be really tough, especially when it leads to conflicts in your relationship. It’s understandable to want to escape those overwhelming feelings. Can you share more about how those choices make you feel afterward?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Afterward, it’s like... I just feel worse. I mean, in the moment, drinking might take the edge off, but then I’m left with this guilt and shame. I think about how I’ve let things slip again, and it just adds to that pressure I already feel. It’s frustrating because I know I’m not handling things the way I want to, but when I’m in that state, it’s hard to see any other way out. I just end up feeling more isolated and angry, not just at myself but at everything around me.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like the temporary relief you get from drinking is quickly overshadowed by feelings of guilt and shame, which only adds to the pressure you’re already experiencing. That cycle of feeling isolated and angry must be really difficult to navigate. It’s understandable to feel trapped in that state when you’re overwhelmed. When you think about those moments, what do you wish you could do differently?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I wish I could just... handle things better, you know? Like, instead of reaching for a drink or avoiding everything, I wish I could find a way to just face the situation head-on. Maybe talk to someone or even just take a deep breath and try to reset my day. I think I want to feel more in control, but it’s hard to break that cycle when I’m in the moment. I guess I just want to feel like I have options instead of feeling stuck.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you have a desire to feel more in control and to face challenges head-on rather than resorting to avoidance or drinking. Wanting to have options and feel empowered in those moments is completely valid. It can be really tough to break that cycle when you're feeling overwhelmed. What do you think might help you start to explore those options when you find yourself in those challenging situations?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think maybe having some kind of plan or strategy could help. Like, if I wake up late, instead of just giving up, I could remind myself that it’s not the end of the world. Maybe I could set smaller goals for the day or even just take a moment to breathe and reset my mindset. I’ve heard that talking to someone can help too, but it’s hard for me to open up. I guess just having a few tools to lean on instead of going straight to the bottle would be a start. It’s just figuring out how to put that into practice when I’m feeling overwhelmed.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re starting to identify some practical strategies that could help you shift your mindset when things don’t go as planned, like setting smaller goals or taking a moment to breathe. It’s great that you’re considering talking to someone as well, even if it feels challenging. Finding tools to lean on instead of turning to drinking is a positive step. What do you think might be a small, manageable step you could take to start putting one of those strategies into practice?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Maybe I could start with something simple, like setting an alarm for a few minutes earlier so I have a little buffer time in the morning. If I do wake up late, I could try to remind myself that it’s okay and that I can still salvage the day. I think just taking a moment to breathe and reset before reacting could help too. It feels small, but I guess it’s about finding those little wins, right? I just need to remember to give myself some grace instead of jumping straight to frustration.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a really thoughtful plan, Alex. Setting an alarm for a few minutes earlier and allowing yourself that buffer time could definitely help create a more gentle start to your day. It’s also important to remind yourself that it’s okay to have setbacks and to give yourself grace. Recognizing those little wins is a powerful way to shift your perspective. How do you feel about starting with that approach?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel a bit hopeful about it, actually. It’s nice to think that I could have a little more control over my mornings. I know it won’t fix everything overnight, but just having a plan feels like a step in the right direction. I guess I’m just trying to be kinder to myself and not let one mistake ruin my whole day. It’s a bit scary to think about changing my routine, but I’m willing to give it a shot.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s wonderful to hear that you’re feeling hopeful and open to trying something new. Acknowledging that change can be scary but also seeing it as a step in the right direction shows a lot of courage. Being kinder to yourself is such an important part of this process. As you start to implement this plan, how do you think you might remind yourself to be gentle with yourself on those tougher days?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think maybe I could write myself a little note or something to keep in my room. Just a reminder that it’s okay to have off days and that one mistake doesn’t define me. I could also try to take a moment each morning to set an intention for the day, something positive to focus on. I guess having those reminders might help me pause and breathe instead of jumping straight to frustration. It’s about creating a little space for myself, I suppose.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a lovely idea, Alex. Writing yourself a note and setting a positive intention each morning could really help create that space for yourself. It’s a gentle way to remind yourself that it’s okay to have off days and to focus on the positives. Creating those small rituals can be powerful in shifting your mindset. How do you feel about the idea of incorporating those reminders into your routine?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel pretty good about it. It seems manageable, and having something tangible to look at could really help me stay grounded. I think it might make a difference to start my day with a little positivity instead of diving straight into stress. I’m hopeful that these small changes could lead to a better mindset overall. It feels nice to think that I can take steps to be kinder to myself, even if it’s just in little ways.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s great to hear that you’re feeling good about these manageable steps and the potential for a more positive mindset. Starting your day with a focus on positivity can really set the tone for how you approach challenges. It’s encouraging to see how you’re embracing the idea of being kinder to yourself, even in small ways. As you begin to implement these changes, what support do you think you might need to help you stay on track?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think having someone to talk to would really help, like a friend or maybe even a therapist. Just having someone to share my progress with and who can remind me to stay on track could make a difference. I also think it might be helpful to check in with myself regularly, maybe at the end of the day, to reflect on what went well and what I can improve. It’s like having a little accountability, you know? Just knowing I’m not alone in this could really help me stick with it.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a solid plan, Alex. Having someone to talk to and share your progress with can provide valuable support and accountability. Checking in with yourself at the end of the day to reflect on your experiences is a great way to reinforce those positive changes and learn from each day. It’s clear that you’re thinking thoughtfully about how to create a supportive environment for yourself. How do you feel about reaching out to someone you trust or exploring the idea of talking to a therapist?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think reaching out to someone I trust could be a good step. It feels a bit daunting, but I know I have friends who care and would be supportive. Talking to a therapist is something I’ve thought about too, but I guess I’ve been hesitant. It feels like a big step, but maybe it’s worth considering if I want to really work through these things. I just want to make sure I’m not carrying all this weight alone. So, I guess I’m open to the idea, even if it feels a bit scary right now.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s completely understandable to feel a bit daunted by the idea of reaching out, but it’s encouraging to hear that you recognize the support of your friends and are open to the possibility of talking to a therapist. Taking that step can feel big, but it’s also a powerful way to lighten the load you’re carrying. It’s great that you’re considering all your options and thinking about what might help you feel less alone. As you move forward, what do you think would be the first step you’d like to take in reaching out for support?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think the first step might be to reach out to a friend I trust. Maybe I could just send a text or call them to see if they’re free to chat. I could share a bit about what I’ve been working on and see if they’re willing to listen. It feels a bit less intimidating than jumping straight into therapy, and I think having that conversation could help me feel more supported. Just taking that first step feels important, and I think it might help me feel more connected.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a wonderful first step, Alex. Reaching out to a trusted friend for a chat can be a great way to feel more connected and supported. It’s perfectly okay to start with a conversation that feels more comfortable for you. Sharing what you’ve been working on can help open the door to deeper discussions and support. It’s clear that you’re taking thoughtful steps toward creating a more positive and supportive environment for yourself. How do you feel about setting a time frame for when you might reach out to your friend?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think setting a time frame could help me actually follow through. Maybe I could aim to reach out within the next couple of days? That way, it doesn’t feel too far off, and I can keep that momentum going. I think having a specific time in mind might make it easier to take that step instead of putting it off. I just want to make sure I don’t overthink it too much. So, yeah, a couple of days sounds good.
